author: robocopsgonemad
content: <@403707149043105803> did you ever elaborate more on the post-quantum designs that can be used with 8141? last i saw was a twitter post.

author: _shemnon
content:
No (a) I've been pulled into government contracting with my daytime work 🤢 but the paychecks keep rolling and the health insurance is in force, so I got that going for me.
(b) for MAYO specifically, I was waiting first for the publication of the NIST "PQC on-ramp" round three results (two weeks ago) and now for the changes deadline before I start structuring a notional precompile and smart contract side, I also have some other day-job distractions.

But to manage expectations, MAYO, if it does get standardized, won't be standardized until 2029 or 2030 or so.  So adding an Ethereum mainnet precompile for it is a bad idea at the moment.

This, however, strengths my mental model for 8141 being the better solution. While MAYO may be wrong for mainnet, it may be perfect for an L2 or Alt-L1 that wants to expose it. But what about the smart contract address or the algorithm number registration that's essential to schemed or CATX transactions?  The only piece of code that needs to care about it is the smart contract code for the SCA address.  The wallet needs to know the signature format and the account address, but it doesn't need to care if it was deployed in a 0x840001 precompile address or in a hashed address.  

i.e. we don't need al algorithm registry if the smart contracts do the routing for us. They know what chain they are set on, and they know the quirks.  CELO's BLS contracts are a great example, they had early version in alternate addresses for years before mainnet finally shipped it.  The wallets don't care, the smart contract papers over the rough edges.

Eliminating the cross-chain algorithm registry table is what really caused me to flip on 8141.  The less required coordination, the better.
